Product Id: The data for 2024 were collected between November 2022 and December 2024. Total household population aged 3 to 79, except those meeting the exclusion criteria (see the Canadian Health Measures Survey (CHMS) Data User Guide: Cycle 7).

In Cycle 7, the weighting calibration process of the Canadian Health Measures Survey (CHMS) was updated to use gender instead of sex at birth. For more information, see the Canadian Health Measures Survey (CHMS) Data User Guide: Cycle 7.

Due to changes in the methodology of the activity monitor component beginning in Cycle 7, caution is advised when comparing data from previous cycles (2007–2009 to 2018–2019) with data released from Cycle 7 (2022–2024) onward.
The sample size for 3 to 5 year olds produces reliable national estimates for both sexes combined only.

Product Id: The Canadian 24-Hour Movement Guidelines recommendations for recreational screen time are: no more than two hours per day for children 5 to 17 years; and no more than three hours per day for adults 18 years and older.

Estimates for this indicator are calculated using the full sample survey weights. However, if combined with the indicator accelerometer-measured sedentary time they are calculated using the activity monitor subsample survey weights. Estimates may differ slightly depending on which survey weights are used.

The Canadian 24-Hour Movement Guidelines recommendations for sleep are: 10 to 13 hours for 3 to 4 year olds (including naps); 9 to 11 hours for children 5 to 13 years; 8 to 10 hours for youth 14 to 17 years; 7 to 9 hours for adults 18 to 64 years and 7 to 8 hours for adults 65 years and older. This indicator is operationalized using the self-reported average daily sleep data and include sleep durations that fall within the recommended range, including any part of the hour (for example 7:00 to 9:59 for adults 18 to 64).

CANSIM Id: On February 3, 2026, an error affecting one adult respondent in the master data file was identified and corrected; the affected estimates have been revised. In addition, an error in the calculation of the lower and upper confidence intervals for the number of persons was identified; the affected estimates have been revised.
